steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding يَ at start and changing vowels shows this happens in the present/future
Adding ونَ shows multiple people are doing the action
Adding كَ shows you are being asked. In this ayah, they are asking about الْأَنفَالِ (the spoils of war)
This is a preposition that shows what they are asking about. In this ayah, it connects the verb يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ (they ask you) to ٱلْأَنفَالِ (the spoils of war).
Added kasra at the end because it's followed by the word ٱلْأَنفَالِ which starts with ال. This makes it easier to pronounce.

steps.m: to give something extra or additional

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make it into a noun meaning the extra thing itself
This form makes it plural, specifically for things that are gained as extras or bonuses
Adding ال makes it refer to specific spoils being asked about

steps.m: of the spoils (of war) (object of عَنِ)
steps.n: The kasra ending shows this word follows the preposition عَنِ (about) when people are asking about it
Changed the root into a command by adding damma (u) and sukun (silent mark) - tells someone to say/speak
The weak letter و is dropped between the two consonants when it has sukun
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to what comes after it in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a basic noun
Changes from single gift (nafal) to plural gifts/spoils (anfaal) by adding 'a' at start
Adding ال makes it refer to specific spoils of war being discussed

steps.m: the spoils of war (subject of question)
steps.n: The ُ shows this is the subject being asked about in يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ (they ask you)
This is the unique name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity and worship, but has become a proper name.
Adding ل at the beginning shows this belongs to or is for Allah. The first ل combines with the ل from الله.
Adding the vowel marks shows this is being used to indicate possession or belonging. The shadda shows the double ل sound.
This pattern is used for someone who does something a lot - in this case, someone who delivers messages
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific messenger
Adding conjunction و to connect with previous phrase
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to لِلَّهِ (for Allah) earlier in the verse
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Added اِتَّ at the beginning to show doing the action to oneself. The ّ (shadda) shows the ت and و combined.
Changed to a command for a group by adding وا at the end and changing the last vowel
Added فَ at start to show this is a result of what was mentioned before. The ٱ is just a different way to write the initial اِ.
Changed to the proper name for God in Islam by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the one being obeyed in وَأَطِيعُوا۟)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل and fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being obeyed in the command 'and obey Allah'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start changes the meaning from doing something to making something happen
Adding وا at the end makes it a command for a group of people
Added وَ at the start to connect this command with the previous one
This is a special word that shows possession or essential nature. It starts with ذَا which is the masculine form.
Adding ت makes the word feminine, showing it's describing a feminine thing
Adding fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb أَصْلِحُوا (make right/reconcile)
Changes from verb 'to separate' into noun meaning 'between'
Added kasra at the end because it's being connected to another word
Added كُمْ to show this refers to multiple people being addressed
Changed to present tense form with أ at start and different vowels
Added وا at the end to change it from 'I obey' to a command meaning 'all of you obey!'
Added و at start to connect this command with previous commands in the verse. This command refers to obeying both Allah and His Messenger mentioned right after this word.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows emphasis.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of فَٱتَّقُوا۟ - so fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon in the phrase 'so fear Allah'
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon in the command 'obey Allah and His messenger'
Added هُ to show this is His messenger (referring to Allah)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with 'Allah' in the phrase 'obey Allah and His messenger'
This is a basic conditional particle that connects two parts of a statement. Here it connects 'if' with 'you were' (كُنتُم) to start an 'if-then' statement.
Added kasra (ِ) under the alif to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'in'
Added damma and sukun to make it a command meaning 'Be!'
Added تُ at the end to change from a command ('be!') to past tense ('I was')
Changed تُ (you) to تُم (you all) to show multiple people instead of one person
Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, like changing 'trust' to 'truster'
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many believers
The doubled م adds extra emphasis to show strong belief
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger. The shadda (ّ) shows the strong emphasis.
When we add مَا to إِنَّ, it changes the meaning completely to mean 'only'. This combination is very common in the Quran to restrict something.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action
Makes the noun definite
Makes it plural (more than one believer). This word is the subject (doer) of وَجِلَتْ (became fearful) in this ayah.
This is a relative pronoun used to describe a group of people. It connects two parts of a sentence, like 'who' or 'whom' in English

This is a conditional particle that introduces a time condition. In this ayah, it connects the mention of Allah (ذُكِرَ ٱللَّهُ) with the believers' hearts becoming fearful (وَجِلَتْ قُلُوبُهُمْ). It's like saying 'whenever X happens, Y follows'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels to show that Allah is being mentioned/remembered rather than doing the mentioning. This word appears in the phrase 'when Allah is mentioned'.
Added ال to specify this is THE one true God, and the hamza was dropped because this word is used so often

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of ذُكِرَ - was mentioned)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added ُ to show this is the subject of the verb ذُكِرَ (was mentioned) in this ayah
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: she/it felt sudden fear (subject is قُلُوبُهُمْ - their hearts)
steps.n: The ت at the end shows this is about a feminine subject (قُلُوبُهُمْ - their hearts) in the past tense
The root meaning of turning/transforming becomes a noun for heart, since the heart is seen as something that turns over emotions and states
Changed the internal structure to show multiple hearts. This word is doing the action of trembling (وَجِلَتْ) when Allah is mentioned
هُمْ means 'their' when attached to the end of a noun
This is a time word that introduces conditional sentences. The ا at the end makes it specifically about future or conditional time.
Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before. This links it to the previous description of believers.
This root gives us words about following something or reciting it in order
Added fathas to connect the letters into a past tense verb
Changed the vowels to make it passive - showing something was done to it rather than it doing something
Added ي to complete the passive form for weak verbs
Added the feminine marker ت to show this refers to آيات (verses) which is feminine

This is a basic preposition that shows something is physically or metaphorically above or on top of something else. It's like saying 'on' in English when you say 'the book is on the table' or 'I depend on you'
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting with pronouns, like how in English we might change 'on' to 'onto' before adding more parts
Added هِمْ which means 'them' (for a group of males). In this ayah, it refers back to ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (the believers) mentioned earlier.

steps.m: to show a sign or mark
steps.n: Forms the plural word 'ayat' meaning 'signs' or 'verses'
Root letters combine to make the noun 'sign/verse', with hamza at start
Changes the singular noun to a plural by adding ات
Adds ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in زَادَتْ (increased)
Adds هُۥ to show these are Allah's signs/verses
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
The middle letter ي becomes ا because it's a hollow verb
Added ت at the end to show a feminine subject did the action
Added هُمْ at the end to show who was increased. In this ayah, it refers to their faith being increased.
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed the word to show the idea of faith itself rather than the action of trusting

steps.m: faith/belief (object of زَادَتْهُمْ - increased them in)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is what they were increased in. This word is connected to زَادَتْهُمْ (increased them) in the ayah.
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, upon, or over something else. In this ayah, it connects to رَبِّهِمْ (their Lord) to show what they rely upon.

steps.m: and upon (connects to رَبِّهِمْ - their Lord)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this phrase to what came before it

steps.m: and upon (connects to رَبِّهِمْ - their Lord)
steps.n: Added the alif khanjariyah (ٰ) which is a pronunciation mark showing the long 'aa' sound at the end
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show emphasis, making it mean 'Lord' - someone who nurtures and takes care of others
Added kasra at the end to show this Lord belongs to someone
Added هِمْ at the end to show the Lord belongs to them. This word is what they put their trust in (يَتَوَكَّلُونَ) in this ayah.

steps.m: to trust or depend on something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding تَ at the beginning and doubling the middle letter shows doing the action to yourself or becoming something
Adding يَ at the beginning and ُ at the end shows this happens in the present/future
Adding ون shows multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, they are putting their trust specifically in their Lord (رَبِّهِمْ)
This is a special word used to point to people or things we're talking about. It's like saying 'the ones who' or 'those who'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added يُ at start to make it present tense. The middle letter changes to make it easier to say. The ُ ending shows it's happening now.
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. This word is connected to ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ (the prayer) which comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changes from verb (doing prayer) to noun (one prayer) by adding ة at the end
Adds 'the' (ال) at the start, making it specific/definite - like going from 'a prayer' to 'the prayer'

steps.m: the prayer (object of يُقِيمُونَ - they establish)
steps.n: Adds a fatha mark at the end to show this is the object (the thing being established)
Changes the long alif (ٰ) to a regular alif, but keeps same meaning of 'prayer'
Starting with the basic preposition مِن which shows something coming from somewhere else
Added ما (meaning 'what') and the ن combines with م to make a stronger م sound (shown by ّ)

steps.m: and from what (that اللَّهِ provided them)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous one about prayer, showing they both do prayer AND spend from what Allah gave them

steps.m: to provide with what is needed

steps.n: Added fatha (َ) after each letter to make the basic past tense verb meaning 'he provided'
Changed from 'he' to 'we' by adding نا at the end

steps.m: we provided them (with what was needed) (object of يُنفِقُونَ - they spend)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who received the provision. This word connects to يُنفِقُونَ (they spend) in the ayah, showing what they spend from.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changes from past (he spent) to present (he spends) by adding يُ at start

steps.m: they spend (referring to the believers mentioned as الَّذِينَ)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this is about multiple people. This connects to الَّذِينَ (those who) at the start of the verse, describing what the believers do.
This is the basic form of the word used to point to things or people

steps.m: those (pointing to people far away) (subject of هُمُ)
steps.n: Added ئِكَ to show we're pointing to something far away. This word is the subject that هُمُ (they) refers back to.
This is the basic form of the pronoun that means 'they' or 'them' when referring to a group of males or mixed group
Added damma marks to show this is acting as the subject. This pronoun connects to ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (the believers) to say 'they are the believers'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action
Added plural ending to show multiple believers
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular believers. This word completes the meaning of هُمُ (they are)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Root verb 'to confirm truth' becomes noun 'truth' by doubling middle letter
Adding tanween alif (ًا) at the end makes it work as an adverb describing how genuine the believers are
Starting with the basic particle ل which shows direction or purpose
The shadda shows this ل is merged with another ل from the previous word, making the meaning stronger
Added هُمْ at the end to show this is 'for them' (referring to the believers mentioned in the previous words)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Adds ة (taa marboota) to the root to make a noun meaning 'one step/level' instead of the action of stepping
Changes from one level to many levels by adding ات at the end
Adds double damma to show this is the subject form of an indefinite noun
The long ā sound makes the word stronger and more emphatic

steps.m: to be present, to be near
steps.n: This word is used to show location or possession, like saying something is 'at' or 'with' someone
Added kasra and sukun to make it into a noun meaning the place or state of being present

steps.m: with, at, near (of رَبِّهِمْ - their Lord)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is connecting to what comes after it (their Lord). This makes it work like a preposition.

steps.m: to care for and help grow

steps.n: Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to show this is someone who takes care of others completely
Added kasra to show this lord belongs to something
Added هِمْ at the end to show this is their Lord
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added مَ at start and ة at end to change from 'he forgave' to 'forgiveness' (the act of forgiving)
Added وَ to connect this word to the previous phrase about ranks/levels (درجات)
Added tanween dammah to show this is an indefinite noun
Changed from a root meaning 'to provide' into a noun meaning 'the provision/sustenance' by adding special vowels
Adding the double damma marks makes it mean 'a provision' instead of just 'provision'
Added و with fatha to connect this word to what came before it
Added vowels and ي to make it describe someone or something with this quality
Added ٌ to show this is describing the provision (رِزْقٌ) mentioned in the ayah
This is a comparison particle that shows similarity

steps.m: just as/just like (connects to أَخْرَجَكَ - brought you out)
steps.n: Adding ما makes the comparison more specific and connects it to what follows. Here it connects to how Allah brought the Prophet out.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start and changing vowels makes the verb causative - now someone is making something else exit

steps.m: he made you exit (object of رَبُّكَ/your Lord)
steps.n: Adding كَ shows that 'you' is the one being made to exit. This word is being acted upon by رَبُّكَ (your Lord) in this ayah.

steps.m: to care for and help grow
to care for and help grow
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make them pronounceable

steps.m: one who cares for and helps grow
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) shows this is someone who does this action completely and perfectly

steps.m: the one who cares for and helps grow (doer)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the doer of the action in the sentence
Added كَ to show this is your Lord. This word is doing the action of bringing out (أَخْرَجَ) in this ayah.

steps.m: from (connecting بَيْتِكَ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something starts from, what something is made of, or connects parts of a sentence. Here it connects 'your house' (بَيْتِكَ) to what came before it.

steps.m: from (connecting بَيْتِكَ to what comes before it)
steps.n: Added a small ن (called nun) at the end to help with smooth pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by something (in this case, 'your')
Added ك with fatha at the end to show the house belongs to you. This word comes after مِنۢ (from) in the ayah.

steps.m: to be true/right, to be fitting/proper

steps.n: The doubled letter (shadda) makes it into a noun meaning the actual thing or concept
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: with the truth (describing how Allah brought the Prophet ﷺ out)
steps.n: The kasra shows this word follows the preposition بِ (with/by). This describes how Allah brought the Prophet ﷺ out - with/by the truth.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what follows more certain and adds stress to the statement
Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before it

steps.m: a group (something split off from a whole)
steps.n: Added ي to make it mean a group that has been split off from others

steps.m: a group (being acted upon after إِنَّ)
steps.n: The ًا (tanween fatah) shows this is any group, not a specific one. It's in accusative case because it comes after إِنَّ.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
The shadda (ّ) is added because the next word starts with ال, making the ن sound stronger
The fatha (َ) is added at the end because this word connects to the next word ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (the believers)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from a past action (felt safe) to describe someone who believes, using مُ at the start. The ؤ comes from the original أ.
Adds sound masculine plural ending
Added ال to specify these particular believers, and َ to show this is the subject of لَكٰرِهُونَ (they are hating/disliking)
Basic past tense verb showing someone felt dislike
Changed to show someone who does the action. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) in this case.
Added ون to show this refers to multiple people who dislike

steps.m: surely they are ones who strongly dislike (predicate of إنَّ)
steps.n: Added لَ (meaning 'surely/definitely') at start to make it stronger, and changed the ending to match its role in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he argued back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Added alif after first root letter to show mutual/reciprocal action pattern - meaning two or more people are involved in the arguing

steps.m: he argues back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra

steps.m: they argue back and forth (with someone)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: they argue with you (subject of فِى ٱلْحَقِّ)
steps.n: Added ك to show they are arguing with 'you'. The alif is written as ٰ when it comes after ج. This word is followed by فِى ٱلْحَقِّ (about the truth)
This is a primitive preposition that shows location or topic. It has no derivational root or transformation sequence.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
Adding shadda doubles the middle letter to add emphasis
Added 'the' to make it specific. This word comes after فِى (in/concerning) in the ayah
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word فِى (in/concerning)
Basic noun form showing the meaning 'after'
Added fatha at the end because this word will be connected to something after it

steps.m: after what/that which (connects to تَبَيَّنَ - became clear)
steps.n: Adding مَا changes 'after' to mean 'after what' or 'after that which'. In this ayah, it connects to the word تَبَيَّنَ (became clear), meaning 'after it became clear'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger - 'to make very clear' instead of just 'clear'

steps.m: it became clear (after يُجٰدِلُونَكَ - they argue with you)
steps.n: Adding تَ at the beginning shows the clarity happened by itself. This word comes after 'they argue with you', showing what became clear after the argument.
Starting with the basic comparison particle ك
Adding أنّ (indeed) to ك (like) creates a stronger comparison particle

steps.m: as if/as though (describing how the disbelievers argue about the truth as if they were being driven to death)
steps.n: Adding ما makes the word mean 'just as' or 'as if' and connects it more strongly to what comes next - like adding 'just' to make it more specific
Added basic past tense form with fatha marks
Changed from 'he drove' to 'being driven' by adding يُ at start and changing vowels - makes it happen TO someone instead of BY someone

steps.m: they are being driven/led (subject of كَأَنَّمَا)
steps.n: Added plural ending ون to show multiple people are being driven. This word describes what's happening to the people mentioned in كَأَنَّمَا (as if they)
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects 'they are driven' (يُسَاقُونَ) with 'death' (ٱلْمَوْتِ), showing they are being driven toward death.
Added the vowel marks to show exactly how to pronounce the word - إِ has a kasra (the small line below) and ى has a fatha (the small line above)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun. The و gets a sukun because it's a weak letter.
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite
The kasra is added because this word comes after إِلَى (to/towards) in the phrase إِلَى ٱلْمَوْتِ (towards death)
Added diacritics to show correct pronunciation of the pronoun. هُمْ is a pronoun meaning 'they' for a group of males or mixed gender.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. This pronoun هُمْ is the subject of the verb يَنظُرُونَ (they look) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ
Added ُ at end to show this is a complete present tense verb

steps.m: they look carefully (describing those being driven to death)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are looking. In this ayah, it refers to those who are being driven to death (يُسَاقُونَ إِلَى ٱلْمَوْتِ)

steps.m: when (connecting to يَعِدُكُمُ - He promised you)
steps.n: This is a time particle that refers to past events. Here it introduces the time when Allah made His promise.

steps.m: and when (connecting to يَعِدُكُمُ - He promised you)
steps.n: The و connects this time reference to what came before it, while إِذْ introduces when Allah made His promise
Added يَ at beginning to show 'he' doing something now. The و from the root drops out because it comes between two vowels. The ُ at the end shows this is doing the action.

steps.m: he promises you all (subject of يُرِيدُ)
steps.n: Added كُمُ at the end to show who is being promised (you all). The extra ُ helps connect to the next word smoothly.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Adding 'the' makes it refer to a specific deity
Shows this word is the subject of the sentence
Changed initial hamza to kasra hamza and added sukun to make it a number form
Added alif maqsurah (ى) at the end to make it feminine and show it refers to one of the two groups mentioned in the ayah (ٱلطَّآئِفَتَيْنِ)

steps.m: to move around or circle something

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a noun by adding vowels - now means the action of moving around
Changed from the concept to the doer of the action - someone who goes around
Added feminine marker ة to specify a distinct group
Added ال to make it definite and doubled the ط with shadda
Added dual ending تين to show exactly two groups. The ة changes to ت when adding endings.
This is an emphasis particle that introduces and connects what comes after it. In this ayah, it connects to the word غَيْرَ (other than).
Added ها which refers back to غَيْرَ (other than) in this ayah. This connects the word 'that' to what it's talking about.
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose. This word connects to تَكُونُ (it becomes) in this ayah.
Added كُمْ to show this is happening to multiple people being spoken to. This word is receiving the action of تَكُونُ (it becomes) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show emphasis on middle letter
Added ُ at the end to show it's in regular present tense form
Added ون at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
Added و at beginning to connect this word with what came before it
This is the basic form of the particle that connects parts of sentences, like saying 'that' in English to introduce what someone says or promises.
The shadda (ّ) doubles the ن sound to add emphasis, making it stronger than just 'that'. It's like saying 'that indeed' or 'that for sure' in English.
Added fatha on غ and sukun on ي to make it a noun meaning 'other than'
Added fatha at the end because this word describes ذَاتِ (the one/possessor) in the ayah
This is a special word that shows ownership or having qualities. The feminine form starts with ذَا.
Adding ت makes this word feminine, showing it describes a feminine thing that possesses something
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word is describing الشَّوْكَةِ (the thorns/arms) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form basic noun
Adding definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: the armed force/military might (possessed by ذات)
steps.n: The kasra shows this word belongs to ذات (possessor of) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ
Changes from 'he' to 'she/you' by replacing يَ with تَ
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'
Added كُمْ to show this is directed to multiple people. This word is connected to أَنَّهَا (that it) in the ayah, showing what something is for.
Basic meaning of wanting or intending something
The weak letter و changes to ي and the vowels change to show present tense
Added يُ at the beginning to show present tense and who is doing the action
Added وَ at beginning to connect this word with what came before it. This word is connected to يُحِقَّ (establish) in the next ayah.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject

This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show it's elongated when speaking
Adding لِ changes it from pointing to something near to pointing to something far away
Adding كَ shows that the thing being pointed to is far away
This is the basic emphatic particle that introduces a fact or statement
Adding بِ at the beginning changes 'that' to 'because' - it shows the reason for what was mentioned before
Adding هُمْ shows who the word is talking about - in this case, it refers to those who opposed Allah and His Messenger
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show strong or mutual opposition
Changed from past tense to present tense, with shadda showing strong opposition

steps.m: they strongly opposed (Allah and His Messenger)
steps.n: Added وا to show that a group of people did the action. This word relates to Allah and His Messenger as shown in the ayah.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Added ال (the) and merged it with the word, then added emphasis (shadda) to show this is the one true God. The first letter changed to match the pronunciation.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being opposed in يُشَاقِقِ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being opposed in the phrase 'whoever opposes Allah'
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with the previous one (Allah)
Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon - they are opposing the messenger

steps.m: and His messenger (referring to Allah's messenger)
steps.n: Added هُ (hu) at the end to show it means 'His messenger' - the 'His' refers back to Allah
Added fatha to make the word readable and show it's asking about who does something
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb يُشَاقِقِ (opposes) in this ayah.
Added vowels to make it mean 'he split' in the past tense
Adding alif shows the action is done to/with someone else
Adding يُ at start and changing vowels shows this happens in present/future
Changed the final vowel to kasra because this verb comes after مَن (whoever) in the ayah
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Changed from the general word for 'god' to the special name 'Allah' by adding ال (al) and combining letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object being opposed in شَآقُّوا۟)
steps.n: Added fatha to show Allah is the object being opposed in the phrase 'they opposed Allah'
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
The messenger is receiving the action of being opposed, so we add the -a ending to show he is the one being opposed
The هُ suffix means 'his' or 'him'. This word is the object (in accusative case) of the verb شَآقُّوا which means 'they opposed'.

steps.m: and His messenger (object of شَآقُّوا - opposed/resisted)
steps.n: Added conjunction وَ (and) at the beginning of the word to connect it to the previous word
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. It's like adding an exclamation mark in English!

steps.m: then indeed (connects to previous statement about opposing Allah)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the start shows this is happening because of what was mentioned before - that some people opposed Allah and His Messenger
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
Added ال (the) and merged it with the word, then added emphasis (shadda) to show this is the one true God. The hamza at the start changed to ٱ when ال was added.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of شَآقُّوا - they opposed)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is receiving the action of opposition in this ayah
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed to describe a strong quality. This word describes العِقَابِ (punishment) in this ayah.
The ُ shows this word owns or describes what comes after it (العِقَابِ), forming the phrase 'severe in punishment'

steps.m: to punish in return for something

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning the result of punishment. This word is possessed by شَدِيدُ (severe), forming the phrase 'severe in punishment'.
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite

steps.m: of the punishment (possessed by شَدِيدُ - severe)
steps.n: Adding kasra at the end marks the genitive case
The alif is stretched (ٰ) to show something far away instead of near
Added لِ to make it point to something specific and far away

steps.m: that thing over there belonging to all of you
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show it belongs to or relates to multiple people being spoken to
Changed to command form and added وا at the end to mean 'all of you taste!'
Added هُ to show what is being tasted
Added فَ to connect this command with what came before it
This is a basic particle that introduces a fact or statement. The shadda (ّ) makes the ن sound stronger to emphasize the certainty of what follows.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement, showing that this is an additional fact.

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Past tense verb form showing 'he' did the action of rejecting/disbelieving. Like changing 'reject' to 'he rejected'.
Changes from verb (to reject) to noun meaning 'rejecter'. Like changing 'reject' to 'rejecter'.
Makes the word refer to multiple disbelievers, like adding 's' in English

steps.m: for the people who reject truth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to transform the verb into a noun meaning the result of the action. This word is what is being 'tasted' in the phrase 'taste it' (ذُوقُوهُ)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'fire' (because fire gives light)
Added ال to specify this particular fire (referring to the fire of Hell in this context)

steps.m: the fire (possessed by/belonging to the disbelievers)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this fire belongs to or is for the disbelievers (عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ means 'punishment of the fire')

steps.m: to point to or indicate something

steps.n: The root letters أ-ي-ي combine into أَيّ by adding a shadda (doubling mark) on the ي and a fatha on the أ to make it mean 'which one?' when asking questions
Added damma to show this word is the subject being called
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to call attention, like saying 'Hey!' in English
Added ها to make it a proper way to address people, like saying 'O you all!'
This is a special word that helps point to someone or something we're talking about
Adding ن at the end makes it talk about many people instead of just one

steps.m: the ones who (referring to specific people)
steps.n: Adding ال at the beginning makes it point to specific people we're talking about

steps.m: those who (referring to the believers who are being addressed)
steps.n: Final spelling adjustments: the ا becomes ٱ, we add a shadda on ل, and add a fatha at the end to connect with the next word ءَامَنُوٓا۟ (believed)
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Added alif after first letter to strengthen the meaning
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
This is an indeclinable particle (ظرف) that indicates condition or time. It has no root as it's a frozen form.
Adding vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'he met'
Added تُ at the end to change 'he met' to 'I met' in the past tense

steps.m: you all met (referring to الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟)
steps.n: Added م with ُ to make it specifically for a group (you all). This is addressing the believers (الَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟) mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ال with a shadda to make it specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Added ن at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word describes the people who disbelieved (كَفَرُوا۟) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb form
Added the plural suffix وا with damma before it to show multiple people did the action. This word describes those who were met/encountered (لَقِيتُمُ) in the ayah.

steps.m: to move forward slowly in a group

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past-tense verb

steps.m: advancing (as the object of لَقِيتُمُ - you met)
steps.n: Changed to a verbal noun with tanween to show it's describing how the meeting happened - they met them while advancing
This is the basic negation word that tells us not to do something

steps.m: so/then do not (connected to تُوَلُّوهُمُ - turn away from them)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this command follows from what was said before. In this ayah, it connects to the next word تُوَلُّوهُمُ (turn away from them), making the complete phrase mean 'so do not turn away from them'

steps.m: to turn towards/away or take charge of

steps.n: Added vowel marks and doubled the middle letter to show he did the action of turning
Added تُ to show someone else is doing the turning to you
Added و to make it plural - now means multiple people turning away
Added هُمُ at the end to show who is being turned to
Changed the root into a plural noun meaning 'backs' - the physical place where turning/retreating happens

steps.m: the backs (object of تُوَلُّوهُمُ - turn them)
steps.n: Added 'the' at beginning and َ at end to show this is what's receiving the action of تُوَلُّوهُمُ (turn them)
This is a combination of two particles: و (and) + من (who/whoever)

steps.m: to turn away or turn towards

steps.n: The shadda makes the action stronger, and ى shows it's past tense
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added هِمْ to show who receives the action (them)
Added vowel marks to make the basic noun form meaning 'day'
Added fatha to show this word is being used as a time expression
Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that particular day' being referenced in the context

steps.m: to turn back or follow behind

steps.n: Adding damma vowels changes it from a verb root to a noun meaning 'back/rear'
Added a fatha (small line) at the end to show something is happening to someone's back

steps.m: his back (being turned away in battle)
steps.n: Added 'his' at the end. In this ayah, this refers to someone turning their back away from battle, which is being criticized.
This is a basic exception word that shows something or someone is different from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it introduces exceptions to when turning away in battle is wrong.

steps.m: except (introduces exceptions to turning away in battle)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end completes the word. This is how this exception word is always written in the Quran.

steps.m: to turn/move strongly to the side
steps.n: Adding a double ر (called shadda) makes the turning action stronger

steps.m: to move oneself to the side
steps.n: Adding تَ shows someone is doing this action to themselves

steps.m: moving to the side (as a strategy)
steps.n: Adding مُ at start and ِ in middle shows this is describing someone who does this action as a strategy in battle

steps.m: moving to the side (as a battle strategy)
steps.n: Adding اً shows this describes how someone is fighting - by moving to the side strategically
Changed to show something happened to them rather than them doing it
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to make it a noun meaning 'the act of fighting' (especially mutual combat)
Added لِ at beginning to mean 'for/towards' and added tanween kasra at end to show it's indefinite
Added shadda on ل for emphasis and pronunciation

Basic coordinating conjunction giving choice between two options. In this ayah, it connects two valid reasons for tactical retreat: either repositioning for battle (مُتَحَرِّفًا) or joining another group (مُتَحَيِّزًا).
Root contains weak letter و
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed و to ي and doubled it (with shaddah) to show lots of movement
مُ shows this describes someone doing the action, ت shows they do it to themselves
Added ًا to show this word is describing how something is happening
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to فِئَةٍ (a group) to show movement towards that group.
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is a spelling variation of regular alif. This doesn't change the meaning but shows it's at the end of a word.
Basic meaning of root
Changed the root into a noun for a specific group by adding vowels and ة. The hamza (ئ) appears because of the root letter أ.
Added tanwin kasra to show this word comes after the preposition إِلَىٰ (to) in the phrase 'to a group'
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. قَدْ is used to emphasize that something definitely happened or will happen.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before. In this ayah, it connects to the previous condition about someone turning their back in battle.
Basic meaning of returning to a place or incurring something (usually consequences)
Added vowel marks (fathas) to change from the root letters to a past tense verb meaning 'he did it'
The middle weak letter و changed to ا with hamza due to a common Arabic rule for hollow verbs. This word follows فَقَدْ (then certainly) in the ayah to show what happened.
Changes from the action of being angry to the feeling of anger itself
Changes from verb root to noun showing the state of being angry

steps.m: with anger (connected to بَآءَ - he returned)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show 'with' and tanwin kasra at the end. This word explains how the person 'returned' (بَآءَ) - they returned with Allah's anger.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word (اللَّهِ)
Added shadda for emphasis and smoother pronunciation when connecting with the next word
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one and only God
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and alif became a wasl form (ٱ) because it comes after another word
The base form of the verb means to give shelter to someone
Adds و to connect this word with what came before it in the ayah

steps.m: and his place of refuge (subject of جَهَنَّمُ)
steps.n: Adds هُ (meaning 'his') to show it's his shelter. This word connects with 'Hell' later in the verse to say 'his shelter is Hell'
This is a proper noun for Hell, borrowed from Hebrew 'Gehinnom'. Added vowel marks and shadda to show correct pronunciation.
Added the ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject of the verb وَبِئْسَ (and evil/wretched is) in this ayah.

steps.m: to be bad, evil, or miserable
steps.n: This form makes it a noun meaning the quality of the root
Changes it into a special verb used to criticize something

steps.m: and how evil! (connecting this disapproval to what came before)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect this expression of disapproval with the previous statement
Added مَ at the start to turn the verb (to travel) into a noun meaning the place/destination where something ends up
Added ال to make it specific
Added ُ to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added مْ to لَ to create the negative particle that makes verbs past tense. This will affect the verb تَقْتُلُوهُمْ (you killed them) that comes after it.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what happened before. This particle connects this phrase to the previous events.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense with تَ prefix and vowel changes to show 'you' as the doer
Added و at the end to make it plural - means multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: you all did not kill them (فَلَمْ negates and makes past tense)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who is being killed. This word comes after فَلَمْ which makes it negative past tense ('did not kill')
Added shadda and kasra to make the word stronger and add emphasis

steps.m: and yet/however (connecting to قَتَلَهُمْ - killed them)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this contrasting statement with the previous phrase
Changed إِلَه to اللَه, which is a unique name specifically referring to The One True God in Islam
The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence
This is the basic meaning of the root letters
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is something that happened in the past. This word shows what Allah did, as mentioned in the ayah.
Added هُمْ at the end to show who received the action (them). The full meaning shows that Allah is the one who killed them.
This combines وَ (and) with مَا (not) to negate what comes after it
This is the basic meaning of the root letters
Connected the root letters and added vowels. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of a past tense verb.

steps.m: you threw (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Added تَ at the end to show that 'you' did the action. In this ayah, Allah is addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ about when he threw dust at the enemy.

steps.m: when (connects رَمَيْتَ before and after it)
steps.n: This word connects two instances of رَمَيْتَ (you threw) in the ayah, showing when one action happened in relation to the other. It's like saying 'at the time when you threw'.
This is the basic meaning of the root letters
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' (masculine singular) did the action
This is the basic form of the particle that shows contrast with what came before
Adding shadda and fatha at the end makes the meaning stronger
Adding و at the beginning connects this contrasting statement with what came before it
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for the one true God. The hamza is dropped and the first lam gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of the sentence)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon
Basic past tense form with fatha vowels. The final ى is due to the weak letter root ending.

steps.m: he threw (past tense, subject of قَتَلَهُمْ)
steps.n: Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound. This word is connected to قَتَلَهُمْ (he killed them) in meaning, as both actions are attributed to Allah in this verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels, changing the final و to ا
Added ي to make it present tense

steps.m: so that he tests (purpose of رَمَىٰ)
steps.n: The لِ prefix shows purpose - this testing is the purpose/result of Allah's throwing mentioned in رَمَىٰ

steps.m: and so that he tests (purpose of رَمَىٰ)

steps.n: Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, like changing 'trust' to 'truster'

steps.m: many who trust/believe (being tested by يُبْلِىَ)
steps.n: Added ين to show we're talking about many believers. This word is receiving the action of testing (يُبْلِىَ) in this ayah.
Added ٱلْ to show we're talking about specific believers, and َ at the end because it's receiving the action of testing
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added the pronoun هُ (him/it) to show what/who the word 'from' refers to. Here it refers back to the trial (بَلَآءً) mentioned in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the result of testing

steps.m: a test/trial (object of لِيُبْلِىَ - so that He may test)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being acted upon. The word is being used to describe what kind of test Allah is giving to the believers.
Adding vowel marks changes this into a common adjective meaning 'good'
Added ًا to show this describes the test/trial (بَلَآءً) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes that اللَّهَ is سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ)
steps.n: Adding shadda (ّ) changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. It makes what comes after it more certain and important. Here it emphasizes that Allah is All-Hearing and All-Knowing.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added a long vowel mark (small alif) after the second letter to extend the sound
Added 'al' to make it definite - 'the god' instead of just 'a god'
The word 'Allah' comes from 'al-ilah' (meaning 'the god'), but is a special name. It gets a fatha mark at the end because it follows inna (indeed).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who hears perfectly (attribute of اللَّهَ)
steps.n: Changed the middle vowel to kasra and added ي to show this is someone who hears perfectly
Added two dots (tanween) at the end to show this is a complete attribute of Allah

steps.m: One who knows completely (attribute of اللَّه)
steps.n: The pattern with ي shows this is a permanent and complete quality. When used for Allah, it means His knowledge is perfect and complete.

steps.m: All-Knowing, the Perfectly Knowing One (attribute of اللَّه)
steps.n: The tanween shows this is one of Allah's established attributes mentioned independently
This is the basic demonstrative word used to point to things that are close by, like saying 'this' in English
Adding ل with a long alif (ٰ) and kasra (ِ) changes 'this' to 'that', making it point to something far away instead of close by

steps.m: that (pointing to something far away) belonging to all of you
steps.n: Adding كُمْ at the end shows this belongs to or relates to multiple people being spoken to
This is a special word that makes you sure about what comes after it, like saying 'indeed' or 'that' in English
The و at the start connects this word to ذٰلِكُمْ (that) which came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that refers specifically to the One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being referred to)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' gets a stronger L sound (shadda), starts with a connecting alif, and ends with a fatha to show something is happening to Allah in this sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: one who weakens (كَيْدِ the plot of)
steps.n: Added مُ at the start and changed the middle vowel to kasra to show this is about someone who does the weakening. In this ayah, it's specifically about weakening the plots/plans of others.

steps.m: to plan secretly or plot against someone

steps.n: Added vowel marks to turn the action into a noun meaning 'a plot' or 'scheme'

steps.m: of the plot/scheme (possessed by مُوهِنُ - weakener)
steps.n: The ِ at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to مُوهِنُ (weakener), forming the phrase 'weakener of the plot'

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he' form
Changed to active participle form to show who does the action
Added plural ending to show multiple people

steps.m: the ones who reject truth (object of مُوهِنُ)
steps.n: Added 'the' at beginning to make it specific. The word is the object of مُوهِنُ (weakener) in this ayah.
This is a conditional particle that introduces the possible situation of 'if you seek a decision'. It connects this condition to its result which comes after.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Added است at beginning to show seeking or requesting victory/judgment
Changed to present tense by adding ت, showing 'you' as the doer
Changed from singular 'you' to plural 'you all' by adding وا at the end
Added plural marker to show multiple people are doing the action
Added vowel marks to show this is a particle that emphasizes that something definitely happened
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what comes after it (جَآءَكُمُ meaning 'came to you')
Added fathas and changed the middle letter ي to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Added long alif to show extended/complete coming
Added كُمُ at the end to show who was affected by the action (you all)

steps.m: to open, grant victory, judge between

steps.n: Added vowels to make it mean 'he opened' in the past tense
Changes from 'he opened' (action) to 'opening/victory' (name of the action)
Added the definite article ال to specify a particular victory
Added ُ (damma) to show this is the subject of the verb جَآءَكُمُ (has come to you) in this ayah. Also changed اَلْ to ٱلْ which is just a writing variation.
Combines two particles to form a compound conjunction
Added vowels to connect the letters and make the basic past tense form
Added 'in' and 't' at the start to show someone stopped themselves (like saying 'self-stopped')
Changed to present tense and second person (you)
Added plural marker to show many people are doing the action
Added vowel marks to show the correct pronunciation of the basic pronoun
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of the previous statement. This connects to the word تَنتَهُوا۟ (you cease/desist) to show what happens as a result.
Changes from verb meaning 'to be good' into noun meaning 'good/better'
Added double damma to show this word is the main subject describing هو (it) in the sentence.
Added fatha to show basic pronunciation
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it introduces 'if you return' (تَعُودُوا۟)

steps.m: and if (connects this condition to the previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement
Added damma to create basic verb structure
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Added وا at end to show multiple people are being addressed
Basic command form. The middle و is dropped because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)

steps.m: we will return (in response to تَعُودُوا۟ - if you return)
steps.n: Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' as the subject. This word is connected to تَعُودُوا۟ (if you return) in the ayah - showing that if they return to fighting, Allah and the believers will also return.
This is a special word used to strongly deny something happening in the future. In this ayah, it's used to say the disbelievers' forces will never help them.

steps.m: and never (in the future) (negates تُغْنِىَ - will suffice)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. This connects to the verb تُغْنِىَ (will suffice) to say 'and will never suffice.'

steps.m: to be enough or make sufficient

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added تُ at start to make it 'she/it' in present tense, changed middle vowels to show it's happening now
Added a fatha at the end to show this is part of a larger phrase starting with 'that'
Preposition عن (from/about) + suffix كم (you plural)

steps.m: to gather or form a group
steps.n: Basic meaning of a group
Forms a noun for a specific group from the root concept of gathering
Adds nominative case ending to show this word is the subject
Adds possession to show whose group it is. This group is the subject of the verb تُغْنِىَ (will avail/benefit) in this ayah.

steps.m: to be a thing or exist
steps.n: Basic meaning of a thing
Root letters joined together with vowels to make the common noun 'thing'. The hamza sound at the end is written as ء.
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon. The ء changes to ـٔ when followed by ا. This word is what is being affected by تُغْنِىَ (will avail/benefit).
Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation of this conditional particle
Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it
Added vowels to connect the root letters. The ُ (damma) on the middle letter shows this is about something becoming a certain way naturally.

steps.m: it (feminine) became many (referring to فِئَتُكُمْ - your group)
steps.n: Added ت with sukun at the end to show this is about a feminine subject (فِئَتُكُمْ - your group) in the past tense
This is an emphasis particle that connects what comes before with what comes after, making the statement stronger. It's like saying 'for sure' or 'definitely' in English.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. This word introduces the phrase 'that Allah is with the believers'.
Added shadda to emphasize that this is the one and only God

steps.m: Allah (as the object being referred to)

Added the vocative particle يا with elongated alif to call attention
Added a small u-sound mark (damma) to make it a proper calling word
Added ها to make it a proper calling word
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' or 'which' when talking about one person or thing
Added ين at the end to make it refer to many people instead of just one
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific, like saying 'the ones who' instead of just 'ones who'
Added final vowel marks and emphasis on ل. This word points to the believers who are being addressed in this verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding alif after hamza changes the meaning from just trusting to actively choosing to believe

steps.m: they believed (referring to the believers being addressed)
steps.n: The وا at the end shows this is talking about a group of people. This word is part of the phrase 'O you who believe' (يٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟) that starts many verses.
This is the basic negation particle that makes actions or statements negative. Here it tells people not to do something (don't betray).
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changed from past tense (he betrayed) to present tense base form (betray) by changing middle and end vowels
Added تَ to show 'you' are doing the action
Added وا to show multiple people are doing the action
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God

steps.m: Allah (as the object of لَا تَخُونُوا۟ - do not betray)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل and fatha at the end to show Allah is being acted upon. The word is the object of the verb 'do not betray' in this ayah.
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Added ال to show we're talking about a specific messenger, and doubled the ر letter because it comes after ال

steps.m: the messenger (being acted upon by تَخُونُوا)
steps.n: Added a fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb تَخُونُوا (do not betray)

steps.m: and the messenger (being acted upon by تَخُونُوا)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous word اللَّهَ (Allah)
The root letters were given vowels to make a past tense verb meaning 'he betrayed'
Adding تَ at start changes 'he betrayed' to 'you betray' (present time)
Added plural marker to show multiple people are doing the action
Added conjunction at beginning to connect with previous phrase
Added an alif after first letter and changed ending to make the root word into a noun meaning 'security'

steps.m: a specific trust (something given to keep safe)
steps.n: Added ة to show one specific instance of trust

steps.m: trusts (multiple things given to keep safe)
steps.n: Added ات to make it plural, showing multiple things given in trust

steps.m: your trusts (object of تَخُونُوا - do not betray)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show these trusts belong to the people being addressed. The word is being acted upon by the verb 'do not betray' in the verse.
This is a pronoun that means 'you' when talking to more than one person. The ْ (sukun) and ُ (damma) help with pronunciation.
Adding وَ at the start connects this word to what came before it. This word is the subject (doer) of تَعْلَمُونَ (you know) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and changed vowels to show present tense for 'you'
Added ون to show that multiple people are being addressed
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he knew' to a command 'know!' by adding alif at start and changing the middle vowel from 'i' to 'a'
Added وا at the end to make it address many people instead of just one
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect it with what came before
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain
Adding ما changes this from just emphasis to emphasizing a specific thing or action that follows
The alif is written long to show the end of the word before the next part of the sentence about your wealth (أَمْوٰلُكُمْ)
Root letters م-و-ل changed to مَال (māl) - the و changed to ا (alif) to make a noun meaning 'wealth'
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple properties/wealth. The و replaces the ا from the singular form.
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added كُمْ to show the wealth belongs to multiple people being addressed. The ُ before كُمْ shows this is the subject of the sentence.
Root meaning 'birth/offspring' became a simple noun meaning 'child'
Plural form made by adding أ at start and changing middle vowels - a common pattern for plurals
Added و at beginning to mean 'and'
Added ُ to show this is a subject
Added كُمْ at the end to show these are 'your' children (speaking to many people)
Changes verb concept into noun by adding kasra on first letter and sukun on middle letter
Adds ة to show this is one specific instance of testing/trial
Adds double damma to show this word is the subject in this sentence
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. It's like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English.

steps.m: and that/and verily (connects to previous statement about wealth and children being a trial)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement to what came before it. This links the two ideas: first about wealth and children being a trial, and then about Allah having a great reward.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns
Added ال and a shadda to make it specifically refer to The One God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of knowing in وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟)
steps.n: Added a fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being known in this sentence. The alif also changed to a wavy alif (ٱ) which is just a writing variation.
This is a common preposition in Arabic that comes from the idea of being present with something
Adding vowel marks to the root letters to make a word meaning 'near' or 'at'
Adding fatha at the end to prepare for connection with next part
Adding pronoun suffix to show who/what is being referred to. This word connects to أَجْرٌ (reward) in the ayah, showing that the reward is with Allah.
Changes from root letters into a simple noun meaning reward
Added tanween damma at the end to show this is talking about a general reward. In this ayah, it's specifically referring to the reward that is with Allah.
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a quality or characteristic. This word describes أَجْرٌ (reward) in this ayah.
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite - meaning 'a great (reward)' rather than 'the great (reward)'
Combined the three root letters and added a shadda (double letter mark) to make the question word 'which/who'
Added damma to show this word is the subject being addressed
Added ها to make this a word used for calling attention to someone
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to call attention, like saying 'Hey!' in English
This is the basic form meaning 'who'. It's a special word used to point to people or things we want to describe.
Adding ين at the end makes it refer to many people instead of just one person
Adding ال at the beginning makes it more specific, like saying 'those who' instead of just 'who'
Final spelling adjustments: the ال becomes ٱلَّ with a shadda, and a fatha is added at the end because this word connects to the verb ءَامَنُوٓا۟ (believed) that comes after it
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show past tense
Added alif after first letter to show stronger meaning
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word describes 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) in the ayah.
This is a conditional word that shows what happens next depends on something. Here it connects 'if you have taqwa' with the results that follow.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed to present tense with ت prefix for 'you'
Added reflexive ت that combines with first ت to make تّ (shown by shadda)
Added plural marker وا to show multiple people are doing the action
Changed from 'a god' to 'The God' - this is a unique word used only for Allah
Added the fatha at the end to show Allah is the object of the verb 'fear/be mindful of' (تَتَّقُوا۟). Also added shadda on lam and changed alif to wasl alif for pronunciation.
Added vowels to make it 'he made' in the past tense

steps.m: he makes/sets (in response to تَتَّقُوا - if you fear)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. This shows what Allah does in response to people fearing Him.
This is the basic particle that shows direction or purpose
The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis and indicates this merges with a similar sound from the previous word
Added كُمْ at the end to show this is directed to multiple people being spoken to

steps.m: to separate between truth and falsehood
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of separating or making a distinction
Changed from action to the result of that action

steps.m: a clear standard for distinguishing truth from falsehood
steps.n: The suffix ان creates a noun that represents something that powerfully achieves the root meaning - in this case, something that helps distinguish truth from falsehood

steps.m: a criterion (object of يَجْعَل/make for you)
steps.n: The ًا shows this is what Allah will make/set (يَجْعَل) for the believers as mentioned in the ayah
The basic meaning relates to covering something
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning more intense - showing complete removal
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at the start and putting a kasra (i sound) in the middle

steps.m: and he removes completely (the sins from عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ)
steps.n: Added و to connect this word to what came before it in the ayah
This is a preposition that shows something moving away from or separating from something else

steps.m: away from you all (object of يُكَفِّرْ)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كُمْ to show who is being moved away from. This word is receiving the action of يُكَفِّرْ (he removes) in this ayah.
This root relates to anything that is bad or evil

steps.m: very evil or harmful (ready to connect)
steps.n: Changed to show something is very evil/harmful by doubling the middle letter and adding vowels
Adding ات makes it plural, showing we're talking about multiple bad actions or deeds
Adding ِ shows this word belongs to or connects with something else
Added كُمْ to show these bad deeds belong to 'you all' (the believers being addressed)

steps.m: to cover up faults or sins
steps.n: The basic meaning is to cover up sins or faults
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present/future tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he will forgive (in response to تَتَّقُوا)
steps.n: Changed the ending to sukun to show this is part of a conditional statement - it will happen if you fear Allah

steps.m: and He will forgive (in response to تَتَّقُوا)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement about Allah making a criterion for you
Added fatha to show this is connecting to the verb يَجْعَل (he makes/places). The ل shows who benefits from the action.
Added كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. The damma (ُ) shows it's attached to the end of another word, and the sukun (ْ) shows it's the end of the word.
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity and worship, but has become a unique proper noun.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation
Added damma at the end to show Allah is the subject doing the actions in this verse (having great bounty)
Added و at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement, plus alif with hamza wasl (ٱ) which helps pronunciation
This is a special word that doesn't come from a typical root. It's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic.

steps.m: possessor of, owner of (describes الله in this ayah)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) to show this is the subject form. This word is describing Allah in this ayah, showing He is the possessor of great bounty.
The basic meaning is about having extra or being more than needed
Changed the root into a noun meaning the result of giving extra/favoring. This word belongs to ذُو (owner of) in this ayah.

steps.m: the favor, the bounty (possessed by ذُو)
steps.n: Adding definite article makes the noun definite
Adding i-sound at the end because it's 'of the' favor
Added the ي between second and third root letters to show this describes a quality
Added ٱلْ to make it definite and ِ at the end because it describes Allah who has ِ in this ayah
This is a time word that shows when something happened in the past. Here it introduces when the disbelievers were plotting.
The و connects this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English. It shows this is another event being mentioned.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, and damma on last two root letters. In this ayah, this plotting is being done against the Prophet (you).
Basic preposition showing connection or means. In this context with يَمْكُرُ (plot/plan), it shows plotting against someone.
The كَ means 'you' (when speaking to one male). The word بِكَ shows who is being plotted against in يَمْكُرُ (plot/plan).
This is a complete demonstrative pronoun that is not derived from a root. It consists of the definite article ال + ذي (who/which) + ن (plural marker) + َ (case ending)

steps.m: to reject, to cover up, to be ungrateful

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: they rejected/disbelieved (referring to those who plotted against the Prophet)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people did this action. This refers to the disbelievers who were plotting against the Prophet in this verse.

steps.m: to restrict, confine, or hold in place

steps.n: Past tense verb meaning 'he made firm/stable' - basic 3 letter past tense pattern
Changes from past tense to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowel to ِ
Changes from singular (he) to plural (they) by adding وا at the end
Adds كَ to show who is being restricted (you)
Adds لِ to show purpose/intent ('in order to')
This is a conjunction that gives choices or alternatives. Here it connects two things the disbelievers were plotting: to kill the Prophet ﷺ or to expel him.
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Adds و at the end to show multiple people (men) are doing the action
Adds كَ to show you (Prophet Muhammad ﷺ) are the one being killed. This is part of what the disbelievers were plotting, as mentioned in يَمْكُرُ (plot/scheme) earlier in the verse.
This is a simple word that connects two choices: 'imprison you' or 'kill you'. It shows these are different options the disbelievers were considering.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to Form IV present tense which makes the action causative - making someone else do the action
Added و to show multiple people doing the action
Added ك to show who is being expelled
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ونَ to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: and they plot/are plotting (connected to يَمْكُرُ in next ayah)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this word with the next ayah where Allah also plots/plans
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on first root letter, damma on second root letter, and damma at end

steps.m: and he plots (connected to يَمْكُرُونَ in previous phrase)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect this word to the previous plotting mentioned in the ayah
Added ال to specify this is THE one true God, and the hamza was dropped because this word is used so often

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَمْكُرُ - He plans)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to wasl-alif since it connects to previous word, and added ُ to show Allah is doing the action in this sentence
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show that Allah is the one doing the action in this sentence
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement, and ٱ (hamzat al-wasl) which helps pronunciation
Changes from verb meaning to noun meaning by adding vowel marks
Added damma ending to show this is describing Allah as being the best of planners in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action

steps.m: those who plan secretly (owned by/of خَيْرُ)
steps.n: Added ين to show multiple people and to show it's owned by the word خَيْرُ (best) in the phrase 'best of plotters'
Added ال to make it specific and known plotters. The alif is written with a special mark (مد) to show it's pronounced longer.

steps.m: when (introduces what happens next in تُتْلَىٰ)
steps.n: This is a time word that introduces what happens next. Here it introduces when 'Our verses are recited to them'

steps.m: and when (introduces what happens next in تُتْلَىٰ)
steps.n: The و connects this phrase to what came before it, while إِذَا introduces what happens next - when 'Our verses are recited to them'
Connected the letters with fatha marks and changed و to ا at the end
Changed vowels to show this is happening to something rather than someone doing it
Changed from past tense to present tense by adding ت and changing the vowels - shows something being recited now instead of before

steps.m: it is being recited (object of عَلَيْهِمْ)
steps.n: Added alif khanjariyah (small alif) over the ى. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning. This word is connected to عَلَيْهِمْ which means it's being recited 'upon them'.
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of or upon something else
Changed ى to يْ to prepare for attaching the pronoun. This is just a spelling change.
Added هِمْ which means 'them' when referring to a group of males or mixed group
The root letters combine with the middle and last letters becoming one long letter (ي). This makes a basic noun meaning 'sign'.
Transforms the root into a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Simple plural form - adding ات to make 'sign' into 'signs'. Like changing 'book' to 'books' in English.
Adds 'our' (نَا) to show these are Allah's signs. The ُ ending shows these signs are the subject of the verb 'are recited'
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter. This is just for pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: they said (referring to the disbelievers responding to Allah's verses)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did this action. This word is part of their response after hearing Allah's verses being recited.
This is a word that adds emphasis to the verb that comes after it (سَمِعْنَا meaning 'we have heard'). It's like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' before a statement in English to make it stronger.

steps.m: to hear or listen to something

steps.n: Added short vowels (fatha and kasra) to make it mean 'he heard' in the past
Added sukun at the end to complete the past tense verb form

steps.m: we heard (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word is part of what 'they said' in the verse.

steps.m: if (hypothetical - introduces what they wish in قَالُوا)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces an impossible or unlikely hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it connects to what they said (قَالُوا) to show what they wished they could do.

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: The root letters ش-ي-أ became شَاءَ (he wanted) by adding vowel marks and changing ي to ا in the middle
Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, and changed the ending to ُ to show it's happening now
Changed to a short command form by dropping the middle letter و and adding a 'u' sound at the start
Adds 'la' at start to make the command stronger/emphasized
Adds 'na' at end to show 'we' did the action

steps.m: surely we would say (in response to their claim)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show 'we' as the subject. This word appears in response to the disbelievers' claim that they could produce something similar to the Quran if they wanted.

steps.m: to be similar or equal to something
similar to (describes what comes after it)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'similar to'. This word introduces what something is being compared to.

steps.m: similar to (object of قَالُوا - what they said)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is the object of what they said (قَالُوا)
This is the basic form of the word that points to something
Added ذا to make it specifically point to something nearby
Added vowel marks and alif sign to help with pronunciation
Changed the final alif to a long alif for emphasis. This word is the subject being described as 'nothing but tales of the ancients' in this ayah.
This is a negative particle that works with إِلَّآ to mean 'nothing/not...except'. In this ayah, it helps say 'this is nothing but tales of the ancients'
This is the basic demonstrative word used to point to things. The fatha helps with pronunciation.
Adding هٰ at the beginning makes it specifically point to something nearby rather than far away. The small alif (ٰ) above the ه is called an alif khanjariya and helps with pronunciation.

steps.m: this (specifically near) (object of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: The long alif (آ) at the end is a pronunciation variant of regular alif (ا). In this ayah, this word is what they said - the object of قَالُوا (they said).
This is the basic exception particle that excludes what comes after it from what was mentioned before.

steps.m: except/only (excluding أَسٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ from what was said before)
steps.n: The alif is written as an extended alif (آ) here. This is just a writing style variation and doesn't change the meaning or pronunciation. In this ayah, it's excluding 'tales of the ancients' from what was mentioned before.

steps.m: to write in lines, to record

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make a simple noun meaning 'one line'
The word for 'line of writing' becomes 'stories/legends' by adding أ at the start and making it plural. The long ā sound after س is written with a small ط above it.
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of إِلَّا (except) in the ayah
