steps.m: he caused to act/made someone do
steps.n: Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Adding هُمْ at the end makes it refer to 'them' (a group of males)
Added fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Changed to common past tense form with alif
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'
This is a basic word that means 'no' or 'not'. Here it negates غَالِبَ (overcomer), making the phrase mean 'no overcomer'
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'
Added ا after first letter and vowel marks to show who does the action. This word is being negated by لَا (no) in this ayah.
Added َ (fatḥa) at the end to show this word is affected by لَا
Added fatha to the letter lam to show it's a preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'

steps.m: for you all (object of غَالِبَ - one who overcomes)
steps.n: Added كُمُ at the end to show this is directed to multiple people. This word connects to غَالِبَ (one who overcomes) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و is a weak letter that takes a sukun.
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific ('the day') and َ at the end to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from' or 'of'. It shows where something comes from or what it's made of, like saying 'made from wood' in English.
Added fatha (َ) at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word ٱلنَّاسِ (the people). This is like a bridge between words to make them flow better when reading.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Forms the noun 'people' from the concept of being social (since people are social beings)
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'
The kasra ending shows this word comes after مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah
This is a word that makes what comes after it stronger or more emphasized, like saying 'truly' or 'definitely'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with what came before it
Added ى at the end to show this is about me (I/my)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter
Added tanween damma to make it into a noun describing someone who does this action
This is a preposition showing possession or purpose
Added the second person plural pronoun to show who is being addressed
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning. This happens because the word comes after و (and) in the ayah.
This is a particle that means 'when' for past events. The shadda emphasizes the meaning.
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this to what happened before. It shows that one thing led to another - when the two armies saw each other, then something happened.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and ا after ر to show people seeing each other (like two groups facing each other)
Changed spelling to show long vowel sound

steps.m: they (fem) saw each other (subject: الْفِئَتَانِ - the two groups)
steps.n: Added feminine past tense ending تِ because the subject (الْفِئَتَانِ - the two groups) is feminine

steps.m: to gather or come together in groups
a group or party (that gathers together)
steps.n: Changes from a root meaning 'gather' into a word meaning 'a gathered group of people'
Changes ة to ت and adds انِ to show exactly two groups (the believers and disbelievers facing each other at Badr)

steps.m: the two groups (the believers and disbelievers)
steps.n: Adds ال to specify these particular two groups mentioned in the battle context

steps.m: to back away or retreat in fear
he retreated in fear (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. In this ayah, this describes how Shaytan retreated when he saw the angels coming to help the believers.
This is the basic form of the word that means something being high or elevated
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word
Added the special ending ىٰ that makes this word work as a preposition. In this ayah, it shows the devil turning back 'upon' his heels.
Added vowels to make the basic noun form meaning 'heel' - the part of foot that follows behind when walking
Added َيْه at the end to show it belongs to him (two of them)
Added possessive pronoun هِ to show these are his heels. The word comes after على (on/upon) in the ayah.
Added fathas to show this is about something that already happened
The middle letter و changes to ا because it has a fatha before and after it. This makes the word easier to say.
Added و with fatha (وَ) at the start to connect this word with what came before it, like using 'and' in English
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English
Added ى at the end to show this is about 'me/I'. The ن gets doubled (shown by ّ) when pronouns are attached. This word connects to جَارٌ (neighbor/protector) to mean 'indeed I am a protector'.

steps.m: to be free from blame or responsibility

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he became free from'

steps.m: one who is completely free from blame
steps.n: Added ي to intensify the meaning, showing complete freedom from blame

steps.m: one who is completely free (indefinite, nominative)
steps.n: Added double damma to show this is indefinite and in the nominative case

steps.m: one who is completely free (indefinite, nominative)
steps.n: Word is already in its final form with the active participle pattern
This is a preposition that shows something coming from or originating from something else
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being talked to
The ن gets a shadda because it comes before ك. This is just a pronunciation change and doesn't affect the meaning.
This is the basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to statements
The نّ gets a kasra (ِ) to prepare for connecting with the pronoun
Added ى with elongation mark (ٓ) at the end to show this means 'I'
Basic meaning of seeing or perceiving with eyes or mind
Changed from basic root 'to see' into 'he saw' by adding vowel marks (fatha) to make it past tense
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to make someone see' instead of just 'to see'

steps.m: I see (what you do not see)
steps.n: Added alif khanjariya (ىٰ) at the end to show the long vowel sound. This word connects to ما لا ترون (what you do not see) in the ayah.

This is a particle that here means 'what' or 'that which' and is being used as the object (thing being seen) in relation to أَرَىٰ (I see). In this context, it's referring to something that others don't see but the speaker does.
This is a basic negative word that makes things opposite, like turning 'can' into 'cannot'. Here it negates غَالِبَ (overcomer) to mean 'no overcomer'
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Added vowels to connect the root letters into past tense form
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' and changed to present tense form
Added ون ending to show multiple people are doing the action. The ى changes to و because of this ending.
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence in English.
Added ي at the end to show this is about me (I). This word connects to أَرَىٰ (I see) in this ayah, showing who is doing the seeing.
Changed the ي to ى with a small alif (ٓ) above it. This is just a writing style change that doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the letters. The middle letter و changes to ا when we add the vowels - this is common with these types of letters.
Added أَ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action in the present time. Added ُ at the end to show this is happening now. In this ayah, the person is speaking directly to Allah.
The word became a proper noun referring specifically to Allah. The hamza was replaced with lam, and the lam got a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being feared in إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being feared in the phrase 'Indeed, I fear Allah'
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. The double ل with shadda is a unique feature of this word.
The ُ (damma) at the end shows Allah is the doer/subject in this sentence
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement, and ٱ (waslah) which is a special form of alif used after و
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to make it an adjective showing strong quality
Added damma at the end to show this describes العِقَابِ (punishment) in this ayah

steps.m: to follow closely or return back

steps.n: Added vowels and alif to make it a noun meaning the consequence or payback that follows an action
Added ال (the) at the start to make it mean 'the punishment' instead of just 'punishment'
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by شَدِيدُ (severe/intense) in the phrase شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (severe in punishment)

steps.m: when (referring to the time when يَقُولُ/they say)
steps.n: This is a time particle that connects to the verb يَقُولُ (they say) in this ayah, showing when they said something in the past
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense form
Added يَ at start to show present tense. The middle و returns and ا is dropped because it's happening now, not in the past. The ُ at the end shows he is doing it.
Added alif between first and second letters to show doing something back and forth (like pretending to be something you're not)
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'someone who' and changed the word pattern to show the doer of the action
Added ال at beginning to make it specific
Added ون at the end to show multiple people and changed alif to make pronunciation smoother. This word is the subject (doer) of يَقُولُ (saying/speaks) in this ayah.
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people
Added ال to make it specific and turn it into 'those who'
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This connects this group with the hypocrites (ٱلْمُنٰفِقُونَ) mentioned before.
Basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it shows where something is located - specifically inside their hearts.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Basic noun form - a heart is named this because it constantly turns blood over in the body
Changes from single heart (qalb) to plural hearts (qulūb) by changing the vowels inside the word
Adds هِم at the end to show the hearts belong to them (referring to the hypocrites and those with disease in their hearts mentioned earlier in the verse)
Changed from root letters to a noun meaning 'sickness' by adding the vowel marks a-a
Added tanween (double damma) to make it 'a sickness' instead of 'the sickness'

steps.m: a severe sickness/disease (in قُلُوبِهِم - their hearts)
steps.n: The letter م gets doubled (has a shadda), making the sickness stronger/more intense. This word describes the spiritual sickness in their hearts.
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

The doubled ر (with shadda) shows this was intense deception. This word describes what the hypocrites and those with disease in their hearts are saying about how their religion deceived the believers.
Starting with the basic pointing word 'ha' and adding the alif above to show emphasis
Adding the small alif above to help with pronunciation
Adding ؤُ to show we're pointing to multiple things
Adding لَآ to make it point to things that are either near or far

steps.m: these/those (pointing to the hypocrites and those with disease in their hearts)
steps.n: Adding ءِ at the end to complete the word. In this ayah, it's pointing to the hypocrites and those with disease in their hearts who were mentioned earlier.

steps.m: to follow a way/path or owe something

steps.n: Added kasra under د and long ي to make it into a noun meaning a complete system or way of life
Added damma to connect with next suffix

steps.m: their religion (referring to those who deceived them)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this belongs to them (referring back to those mentioned earlier in the verse)
This is a word that refers to any person. In this ayah, it's the subject (doer) of the verb يَتَوَكَّلْ (relies upon/puts trust in).
The و connects this clause to what came before it, showing this is an additional thought or statement.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Added يَ at the start and ُ at the end to change from past to present tense
Added تَ and doubled ك to change meaning from simple 'trusting' to actively 'relying/depending on' something
Changed from regular present tense to a command form, showing reliance specifically on Allah
This is a helper word that shows something is on top of or depending on something else. Here it shows trust being placed upon Allah.
Added shadda on ل to show emphasis in this divine name
Changed alif to wasl-alif and added kasra at the end to show possession
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. The shadda (double mark) on ن makes the emphasis stronger.

steps.m: then indeed (connects to previous statement about those who trust in Allah)
steps.n: The فَ shows this is a result of what came before - in this case, it's connecting to the previous statement about those who trust in Allah.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being relied upon in يَتَوَكَّلْ)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to show emphasis, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show Allah is the object being relied upon in the phrase 'whoever relies upon Allah'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters and changed vowels to make an intensive adjective showing a strong quality
Added tanween dammah to make it a standalone description. In this ayah, it's used as one of Allah's names/attributes.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: wise, full of wisdom (describes اللَّهَ - Allah)
steps.n: Changes the verb into an intensive adjective showing someone who has great wisdom. In this ayah, it describes Allah, showing that He is perfectly wise in His decisions.
Added vowel marks to show how the word is pronounced. لو is a conditional particle that introduces hypothetical situations.
Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it

steps.m: to see/observe with one's eyes or mind

steps.n: Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he saw'
Changed from 'he saw' to 'you see' by adding ت prefix
Added vowel mark to help with pronunciation
Added alif khanjariya (ٰ) and a small alif (ٓ) to show the long vowel sound

This is a time particle that connects two events in the past. Here it connects 'if you could see' (تَرَىٰٓ) with 'the angels take' (يَتَوَفَّى), showing when one action happens in relation to the other.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Added present tense prefix and doubled middle letter for emphasis
Added ت after يَ to show the action affects the doer
Starting with the basic demonstrative word and changing it to make it relative
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Adding the shadda on ل and kasra under ذ helps with pronunciation
Adding ين makes it refer to a group instead of one person. This word describes the people who disbelieved (كَفَرُوا۟) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb. This word describes the people who the angels are striking in this ayah.
Added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action. The small و mark (۟) above is just to help with pronunciation.
Changed the root letters into a noun by adding the vowels 'a' to mean 'one who has power' (angel)
The singular 'angel' becomes plural by adding alif and changing the middle vowels - a common plural pattern in Arabic
Added ة to create collective plural form
Added the definite article ال and nominative case ending ُ to show this is the subject doing the action of taking/receiving
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they hit/strike (the angels strike their faces)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple angels are doing the action. This refers to the angels (الْمَلٰٓئِكَةُ) striking the faces of the disbelievers.
Basic root meaning related to facing or directing towards something
Root letters combined to make the noun meaning 'face'
Changes from singular 'face' to plural 'faces' by changing middle vowels to damma (u sound)
Adds fatha to show this is the object being struck (by يَضْرِبُونَ - they strike)

steps.m: their faces (object of يَضْرِبُونَ - they strike)
steps.n: Adds plural possessive pronoun meaning 'their'
Basic vowels (fatha) added between letters to make past tense verb meaning 'he turned back'
Changed from a verb 'to turn back' into a plural noun 'backs' by adding أ at start and ا after ب
Added conjunction 'and' at beginning

steps.m: and their backs (object being struck by يَضْرِبُونَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at end to show these are their backs. This word is being acted upon by يَضْرِبُونَ (they strike) in this ayah.

steps.m: to taste or experience something directly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he tasted' to a command 'taste!' by changing the middle vowel from 'a' to 'u'
Added وا at the end to make it a command for a group

steps.m: and all of you taste!/experience! (the punishment of burning)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect it to previous statement. This word connects to عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (punishment of burning) which comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to make it a noun meaning 'punishment'. This word is connected to الحريق (the burning) to mean 'punishment of burning'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective/noun describing something that burns or the state of burning
Added the definite article 'al' to specify this particular burning (referring to the punishment mentioned in the ayah)
Added kasra at the end to show this describes the punishment (عَذَابَ) mentioned in the ayah
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with an overbar (ٰ) when other letters are attached to it
Adding لِ changes the word to point to something farther away instead of nearby

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (refers to what is mentioned in the previous ayah)
steps.n: ذٰلِ becomes ذٰلِكَ by adding كَ at the end. This كَ is like a pointer - it helps show we're talking about something specific that was mentioned before, like when you point to something far away.
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: because of what (relates to قَدَّمَتْ - sent forth)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show causation. This word connects to قَدَّمَتْ (sent forth) to explain why something happened.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling mark) on middle letter makes the action stronger or shows it happened multiple times

steps.m: she sent forward (with emphasis/multiple times) (subject of what أَيْدِيكُمْ did)
steps.n: Adding ت at the end changes the subject from 'he' to 'she'. In this ayah, this refers to what 'your hands' (أَيْدِيكُمْ) did or sent forward.
Forms the basic noun for a single hand
Changes from one hand (يَد) to many hands (أَيْدِي) using a special plural pattern for body parts
The كُمْ at the end shows this belongs to multiple people being spoken to (in this ayah, referring to the wrongdoers mentioned earlier)
Added fatha and shadda to create the emphatic particle that introduces certainty to what follows
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before. This introduces the statement that Allah is not unjust to His servants.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' by adding vowel marks
Added ٱل (the) at the start and doubled the middle letter to make it specific and emphasize its uniqueness. The َ at the end shows this is the object of an action in this sentence.
This is a special verb used for negation in Arabic. It's irregular and only appears in past tense form but gives present meaning.
Adding the basic vowels to make it a complete word. This is a special verb that only comes in past tense form but means 'is not'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who wrongs others a lot
steps.n: This form shows someone who does the action a lot or as a habit

steps.m: as one who wrongs others a lot (connected to لَيْسَ)
steps.n: The word بِ here means 'as' because it's connected to the word لَيْسَ (is not) earlier in the verse

steps.m: as one who wrongs others a lot (connected to لَيْسَ)
steps.n: The tanwin at the end (ٍ) shows this is indefinite, like saying 'a' or 'any' wrongdoer
This root is about serving or worshipping
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past-tense verb
Changes from singular verb to plural noun meaning 'slaves'
Added 'al' (the) at the start and kasra at the end because it comes after the word 'li'
Adds 'li' meaning 'for/to' before the word

steps.m: to persist or continue doing something
steps.n: This is the basic meaning showing continuous action

steps.m: habit, usual way of doing things
steps.n: Changed from an action to a noun meaning the way something is usually done
Added kasra to show this word is owned by آل (family/people of) in the phrase
Added ك at beginning to show comparison - this means the situation being described is similar to how Pharaoh's people usually behaved

steps.m: to be first, to lead, to govern
steps.n: This root relates to being first or in charge, which connects to the idea of family leadership
Added vowels to make the root letters 'a-w-l' into a word meaning 'first'
These are actually two different words - not a transformation. آل meaning 'family' is a separate word from أول meaning 'first'
Added kasra at the end to show this word connects to what comes after it (Pharaoh)
This is a borrowed word from ancient Egyptian, so it doesn't have an Arabic root
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly

steps.m: Pharaoh (being acted upon in كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ - like the way of the people of Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because Pharaoh is the object being referred to in 'like the people of Pharaoh'
Added ن to show we're talking about multiple people
Added ال at the start to make it specific, like saying 'the ones who' instead of just 'who'

steps.m: and those who (connects to كَفَرُوا۟ - they disbelieved)
steps.n: Added و at the start to connect this with what came before. The people being described here are the ones who disbelieved.
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the word قَبْلِ (before) with هِمْ (them) to mean 'before them'.

steps.m: to be in front of or before

steps.n: Changes from root to basic noun meaning 'before'
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by what comes after it
Added هِمْ which means 'their/them', showing who the word 'before' relates to
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Adds masculine plural past tense suffix indicating 'they'
Forms the singular noun meaning 'sign' or 'verse' from the root
Forms feminine plural of آية (sign/verse)
Added preposition بِ to show how Allah took them. This connects to the verb فَأَخَذَهُمُ (then He seized them)
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah) showing these are Allah's signs
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added kasra at end to show this word is 'of' something

steps.m: to seize or take hold of

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to show this is something Allah did in the past
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of what came before

steps.m: so he seized them (subject of اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added هُم to show who was seized - refers back to those who disbelieved mentioned earlier
Added damma at the end to show this is connected to the next word
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
The word for 'god' (إِلَه) becomes the special name 'Allah' (ٱللَّهُ) by adding ال (the) and doubling the ل sound. The ُ at the end shows Allah is doing the action.
Changed from root letters to a noun meaning 'sin' by adding vowels
Changed vowels and added و to show multiple wrong actions
Added هِمْ at the end to show these belong to them
Added بِ at the beginning to show these wrong actions were the reason for something
This is a special word that adds emphasis and makes the word after it end in َ (fatha). Here it emphasizes that Allah is strong. It's like adding an exclamation mark or saying 'truly' in English.
Added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form for smoother pronunciation

steps.m: Allah (as the object of كَفَرُوا - they disbelieved in)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being disbelieved in
Added vowels to connect the letters and make an adjective. The َ (fatha) and ِ (kasra) pattern shows a quality or characteristic.
Added ٌّ (double damma with shadda) to show this is describing Allah's perfect attribute of strength. This word is describing Allah in the phrase إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ (Indeed, Allah is All-Strong).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective by adding ي between the second and third root letters. This word describes how intense the punishment is.
Added damma at the end because this word is describing something that comes after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed the pattern to create a noun meaning the result of the action. The meaning shifted from following to punishment because punishment follows wrongdoing.
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it's owned by the word شَدِيدُ (severe) in شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (severe in punishment)
This is the basic demonstrative word used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) above the ذ
Adding لِ changes this from pointing to something near to pointing to something far

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (introduces the explanation in بِأَنَّ)
steps.n: Adding كَ completes the word. In this ayah, it points to what comes after it, introduced by بِأَنَّ (because...)
This is a basic particle that introduces a new clause and adds emphasis

steps.m: because (connects this reason to ذٰلِكَ - that)
steps.n: Adding بِ at the beginning changes 'that' to 'because', connecting it to the word 'that' (ذٰلِكَ) before it to explain the reason
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added fatha at the end because Allah is the object of أَنَّ (that/indeed) in this ayah. Also changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
This is a negative particle that changes يَكُ (was) into 'was not'. It always makes verbs refer to the past, even if they look like present tense.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
The word is shortened because it comes after لَمْ which requires jussive mood. The و and ن are dropped in this special case.
Added shadda to show intensive change and connected letters with fatha

steps.m: one who changes things significantly (object of يَكُ/was not)
steps.n: This word is the object of يَكُ (was not) in the phrase لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا (was not one who changes)
Root letters put together to make the noun form meaning 'blessing'
Changed the first vowel and added ة to make it refer to one specific instance
Added emphasis to show the greatness of the blessing
Added tanween to show this is the object receiving the action in أَنْعَمَهَا (He bestowed it)
Adding أَ at the start makes it mean 'to cause/give blessings'. This is connected to يَكُ (was) in the ayah.

steps.m: he bestowed it (referring to نِّعْمَةً - blessing)
steps.n: Adding هَا refers back to نِّعْمَةً (blessing) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Basic form of the word expressing the concept of being high or elevated
Adding ى changes this from a concept into a preposition that shows one thing being on top of another
The small alif (ٰ) helps show this is connected to the word قَوْمٍ (people/folk) that comes after it in the ayah

steps.m: to stand upright, rise up, establish

steps.n: Changes the verb into a collective noun for a group of people who stand together as one community
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this word is being possessed by or connected to something else in the sentence. It comes after على (upon) in this ayah.
Basic particle form indicating endpoint
Adding the shadda emphasizes the meaning
The ىٰ at the end shows this connects to the next word يُغَيِّرُوا۟ (they change). Together they mean 'until they change'
Added fatha marks and doubled the middle letter to show strong/intensive change
Changes from past tense (he changed) to present tense (he changes) by adding يُ at the start and changing the ending vowel

steps.m: they change (until they change what is within themselves)
steps.n: Changes from 'he changes' to 'they change' - the verb now shows multiple people doing the action. Connected to حَتَّىٰ to show something must happen first.
This is a connecting word that links what comes before it with what comes after it. Here it connects the changing (يُغَيِّرُوا۟) with the blessing (نِّعْمَةً) to show what is being changed.
Changes the singular 'self' into a plural form meaning 'selves' or 'souls'
Adds kasra at the end to indicate genitive case
Adds بِ at the beginning to show how something is done or the means by which it happens
Adds the third person plural possessive pronoun
This is a particle that introduces and emphasizes what comes after it. It's like saying 'indeed' or 'certainly' before a statement.

steps.m: and that (connects this emphasized statement to اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this statement to what came before it
Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object being described in this ayah)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' gets a special mark (shadda) to make the L sound stronger, and has a short 'a' sound at the end to show it's being described in this sentence

steps.m: one who hears intensely (attribute of اللَّه)
steps.n: The ي between م and ع shows this is a strong, permanent quality. This word describes Allah's attribute of hearing everything.
The tanween (ٌ) shows this is one of Allah's names/attributes being used generally. This pairs with عَلِيمٌ (All-Knowing) to show Allah's complete awareness.
Added vowels and ي to show this describes someone who knows everything completely. When used for Allah, it means He has perfect and complete knowledge of all things.
The tanween (double damma) shows this is describing Allah in a general way, emphasizing that being All-Knowing is part of His nature.

steps.m: to persist or continue in a way/habit
way or habit (possessed by آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun by adding vowel marks. This word describes the characteristic way or habit of Pharaoh's people.
Added كَ at the beginning to mean 'like' and ِ at the end because it's connected to the next word آلِ (family/people of)
This root relates to being first or leading, which connects to the concept of family/followers since they follow their leader
Added vowels to make root letters 'a-w-l' readable as 'first'
Added long alif (آ) to change meaning to 'family/clan'
Added a kasra (i-sound) at the end to show this word belongs to or connects with what comes after it (like saying 'family of')
This is a borrowed word from ancient Egyptian, not derived from an Arabic root. It refers to the ruler of ancient Egypt.
The َ (fatha) at the end shows Pharaoh is receiving the action of drowning in this verse. This connects to the verb أَغْرَقْنَا (we drowned) which comes before it.
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people
Added ال with shadda to make it specific, like saying 'the ones' instead of just 'ones'
Added وَ to connect it to what came before, and added َ at the end because this word is doing the action in كَذَّبُوا (they denied)
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the word قَبْلِ (before) with هِمْ (them) to mean 'before them'.
Adding vowels to make the basic noun meaning 'before'
Added kasra to show this word is owned by what comes after it
Added هِمْ which means 'their/them', showing who the word 'before' relates to
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he strongly denied/rejected (بِـَٔايٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ - the signs of their Lord)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows doing something with intensity or repeatedly. In this ayah, it refers to their strong rejection of Allah's signs.

steps.m: they strongly denied/rejected (بِـَٔايٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ - the signs of their Lord)
steps.n: Adds third person masculine plural ending to show multiple subjects

steps.m: a sign (something that shows or proves)
steps.n: Changes the basic idea of showing into a noun meaning 'a sign' - something that shows or proves something

steps.m: signs (multiple things that show or prove)
steps.n: Makes the word plural to show many signs
Adds بِ at the beginning to show these signs are being used as tools or means (connected to كَذَّبُوا۟ - they denied)
Adds kasra at the end because this word is connected to رَبِّهِمْ (their Lord) that comes after it

steps.m: Lord (the one who cares for everything)
steps.n: The doubled letter in the middle shows this is someone who does a lot of caring and nurturing

steps.m: their Lord (referring to those who denied Allah's signs)
steps.n: Adds هِمْ at the end to show it belongs to them. In this ayah, it refers to the people of Pharaoh and those before them who denied Allah's signs.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding hamza prefix changes from 'to perish' to 'to cause to perish/destroy'. The middle letter gets a sukun.
Adding نا at the end changes 'he destroyed' to 'we destroyed'
Adding فَ shows this action happened as a result of something before it
Adding هُم shows who was destroyed (them)
Root letters turned into a noun meaning 'sin' by adding the vowels َ and ْ
Makes the word plural by changing internal vowels and adding و
Adds the preposition ب meaning 'because of/due to'
Adds possessive pronoun suffix meaning 'their'

steps.m: to sink or submerge something in water
to cause to drown (acting on آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ - the people of Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added أ at the beginning to show Allah is making someone else drown. The word آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ (people of Pharaoh) is what's being drowned.
Added نَآ at the end to show 'we' did the drowning. The آ makes the sound longer to emphasize the action.
Added وَ (and) to connect this action with فَأَهْلَكْنٰهُم (so we destroyed them) in the previous verse

steps.m: to be related to or belong to a family

steps.n: The root letters changed to make a special word meaning 'family/people of'. When we see ءَالَ with another name after it (like Pharaoh here), it means 'the family or followers of' that person.
This is a proper noun used as the title for ancient Egyptian rulers. It doesn't come from an Arabic root.
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly

steps.m: Pharaoh (as the object being acted upon in this sentence)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence - specifically, Pharaoh's people were destroyed and drowned (فَأَهْلَكْنٰهُم and وَأَغْرَقْنَآ)
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with the previous phrase about Pharaoh's people (ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ)
Added tanween dammah (ٌ) to show this word is the subject of the next verb كَانُوا (they were)

steps.m: to exist or be in a state

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make it past tense
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has weak middle letter)

steps.m: they were (describing ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَٱلَّذِينَ - the people of Pharaoh and those)
steps.n: Adding وا makes it refer to a group of people (they) instead of one person (he). This refers back to the people of Pharaoh mentioned earlier.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
This pattern shows someone who does the action - like adding '-er' in English (teach → teacher). The word is in accusative case because it is the predicate (khabar) of كان.
Makes the word plural and shows it's affected by كَانُوا (they were)
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger or more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that those who disbelieve are truly the worst of creatures in Allah's sight.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more intense

steps.m: to move or crawl (especially on ground)

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this happens with intensity or repetition
Changes from verb (to crawl) to noun (creature that crawls)
Plural form made by adding و and doubling the ب at the end

steps.m: the creatures that move/crawl (in relation to عِندَ - near/with)
steps.n: Added ال at beginning to make it definite and kasra at end because it follows عِندَ (near/with)
This word is used to show location or possession, like saying something is 'at' or 'with' someone
Adding vowel marks to create the preposition that shows location or possession. In this ayah, it shows something is 'with/at/near Allah'
Added ال to specify this is THE one true deity, and the hamza was dropped because this is a special divine name
Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added kasra at end to show this word belongs to or connects with something else
This is the base form used for referring to someone or something
Adding ين makes it refer to many people instead of just one
Adding ال makes it specific and points to particular people. The ل gets a shadda because ذ is a sun letter that makes the ل sound blend into it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adding masculine plural past tense suffix وا to indicate 'they'
Added damma and sukun to show this is the basic form of 'they'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. This word is the subject (doer) of لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (they do not believe).
This is a negative particle that makes the next verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe) into 'they do not believe'
Added fatha mark to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added a long 'aa' sound at the start to change from 'feeling safe' to 'having faith/believing'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. This word is negated by لَا (not) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to connect descriptions to people or things
Added ال at the beginning to make it definite, and the ل gets a shadda when connecting to ذ
Changed to plural form by adding ين at the end. The initial alif also changes to a wasl form (ٱ). This word is the subject of يَنقُضُونَ (they break) in this ayah.

steps.m: to make a promise, covenant, or agreement

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed the first vowel to a long alif (ٰ) and added sukun at the end to prepare for suffix
Added تَّ (with shadda) at the end to show 'you' did this action. This word connects to مِنْهُمْ (with them) in the ayah.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation - kasra under م and sukun on ن

steps.m: from them (who you made a treaty with)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُمْ (them) to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to those who you (Muhammad ﷺ) made a treaty with.
This word connects what happened before (making a treaty) with what happens next (breaking it). The shadda (ّ) makes the م sound stronger to emphasize the connection between these events.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adding يَ at start and changing vowels shows the action happens in present/future. The middle letter gets ُ (damma) in this pattern.
Adding ونَ shows multiple people are doing the action. This refers to those who you (Muhammad) made a treaty with.

steps.m: to promise, make an agreement, or covenant

steps.n: Root letters were given vowels to make a noun meaning 'promise' from the basic idea of promising
Added fatha at the end to show this is the direct object (the thing being acted upon)
Added attached pronoun هُمْ meaning 'their'
This is a basic preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. It's like saying 'in' or 'inside' in English.
Added ى at the end to complete the word. This preposition connects to the word مَرَّةٍ (time/instance) to show 'in every time/instance'.
Added damma and doubled the middle letter to show completeness
Added kasra at the end because it's connected to the word مَرَّةٍ (time) in the phrase 'every time'

steps.m: to pass, move through, or occur

steps.n: Connected the three root letters and doubled the middle letter (shown by shadda) to make the simple past tense verb meaning 'he passed'
Adding ة changes the verb into a noun meaning 'one instance' of the action

steps.m: a time/instance (in كُلِّ مَرَّةٍ - every time)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is part of the phrase 'every time' with the word كُلِّ before it
Basic pronoun meaning 'they' (masculine plural). This word is the subject of يَتَّقُونَ (they fear/guard against) in this ayah.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, showing these actions happen together.
Basic negative particle that negates the verb يَتَّقُونَ (they fear/are conscious of Allah) that follows it
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added reflexive pattern by adding اِ at start and merging ت with ق to form تّ. The و drops because two strong consonants can't start a word.
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing final ى to ي
Added ونَ to show multiple people doing the action. This word is connected to لَا (not) in the ayah.
This is the basic conditional word that shows something depends on something else happening
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from something before it
Added ما which combines with نْ to make مَّا (shown by the shadda). This adds emphasis and makes the condition more general, like saying 'whenever' instead of just 'if'
Added vowels (َ) to make it 'he found/caught' in the past tense
Adding تَ at start makes it mean 'to become' something
Added نَّ (called noon tawkeed) at the end to strongly emphasize 'you will definitely/surely' do the action

steps.m: you definitely catch/find them (in الْحَرْبِ - war)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being caught/found. This word connects to 'in war' (فِى ٱلْحَرْبِ) in the ayah.

steps.m: in (connects حَرْب with what comes before)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. Here it connects the word حَرْب (war) to the previous context.

steps.m: in (connects حَرْب with what comes before)
steps.n: The ي changes to ى at the end of words. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning the action itself
ال at the start makes it specific, like saying 'the war' instead of just 'war'
The kasra shows this word comes after في (in) in the phrase في الحرب (in the war)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter adds intensity to show the scattering was done with force or multiple times

steps.m: then scatter them! (as a command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ regarding enemies in war)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a result of the previous statement. This command follows after 'if you gain dominance over them in war'
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'

steps.m: with them (who will be made an example of)
steps.n: Added هِم at the end to show this means 'them'. In this ayah, it refers to those who will be made an example of in war.
This is a basic word used to refer to people in questions or statements. It's like saying 'who' or 'whoever' in English.
Added a shadda (doubling mark) on م because it comes after the word إِمَّا. This is a pronunciation change that happens when certain words come together.
Adding fatha and sukun creates the noun pattern that means location or direction
Adding fatha at the end makes it work as an adverb showing location

steps.m: behind them (referring to those who break treaties mentioned earlier in the ayah)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ shows who is being referred to - in this case, those who break treaties mentioned earlier in the verse
This is a hope/expectation particle that shows possibility or purpose. Not derived from a root.

steps.m: perhaps they, so that they (subject of يَذَّكَّرُونَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ (them) to show who the hope/expectation is about. This word connects to يَذَّكَّرُونَ (they remember) in the next ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Doubled the ذ letter to show the action is done with more intensity
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
This is the basic conditional particle that starts conditions in Arabic
Adding ما makes the condition more general, like changing 'if' to 'whenever' in English. The ن and م combine to form مَّا with a shadda.
Adding وَ connects this condition to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas.
Changes from past tense 'he feared' to present tense 'you/she fear' by adding تَ at start

steps.m: you definitely fear (addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: The نّ adds strong emphasis to show this is definitely happening. In this ayah, Allah is addressing Prophet Muhammad ﷺ about how to handle when you fear betrayal from a people.

steps.m: from (connects قَوْمٍ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it connects the word قَوْمٍ (people/folk) to the previous part of the sentence.
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'people' - the idea comes from people standing together as one community
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) because this word comes after the preposition مِن (from)

steps.m: to break trust or be disloyal

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun by adding vowels (i and a) and an alif to show the act of betrayal
The weak letter و changes to ي after kasra, but meaning stays same
Added ة to show one specific instance of the action

steps.m: an act of breaking trust (being acted upon)
steps.n: Added ً (tanween) to show this is an indefinite noun - meaning 'a/an act of breaking trust'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed from 'he threw' to 'you throw!' by adding U- at start and changing middle vowel - makes it a command

steps.m: so throw away! (in response to their betrayal)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a response to the situation mentioned before (if you fear betrayal from people). The ٱ is just a different way of writing the same sound as اُ.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns
Added هِمْ which means 'them' to show who the action is directed towards. This refers back to قَوْمٍ (people) mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is the basic form showing the concept of being in a higher position
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition that shows one thing is positioned above another

steps.m: on/upon (preposition showing equal level in سَوَآءٍ)
steps.n: The small alif (ٰ) is added to show this is pronounced with a long 'aa' sound. Here it connects to سَوَآءٍ (equal level) to show something is spread equally across something else.
Changes from verb 'to be equal' into noun meaning 'equal/alike'
The word got a special ending (ٍ) because it comes after 'upon/on' (عَلَىٰ), showing it means 'on equal terms'
This is a special word that adds emphasis and introduces an important statement. In this ayah, it emphasizes the word اللَّهَ (Allah) that comes right after it.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that became the name for the One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of إِنَّ - indeed)
steps.n: Added the fatha at the end to show Allah is the object of إِنَّ (indeed). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes in middle of speech.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُحِبُّ (He loves) into 'He does not love'. It's one of the most basic and common ways to say 'no' or make something negative in Arabic.
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Added fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Two similar letters (ب) next to each other became one letter with a shadda mark (ّ) on top

steps.m: he loves (لا in this ayah makes it 'does not love')
steps.n: Added يُ at the start to make it present tense ('he loves' instead of 'he loved'). The middle vowel changed to a kasra (i sound). When لا comes before it, it means 'he does not love'

steps.m: he loves (لا in this ayah makes it 'does not love')
steps.n: Added the vowel mark 'u' at the end because this verb is in the present tense and nothing comes before it to change its ending
Changed to describe someone who betrays. This word is the object of يُحِبُّ (loves) in this ayah - Allah does not love the traitors.
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple betrayers
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite
This is a negative particle that tells us the following verb يَحْسَبَنَّ (think/assume) should not happen. It's like saying 'do not think' in English.
The و at the beginning means 'and', connecting this negative command to what came before it. Together with the following verb يَحْسَبَنَّ it means 'and do not think/assume'
This is the basic meaning of thinking or considering something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: let him not think (in context of وَلَا)
steps.n: Added emphatic نَّ ending and changed final vowel to fatha because it follows وَلَا (and not) in the ayah, making it a negative command
This is the base word used to connect descriptions to people or things, like saying 'the one who' in English
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people instead of just one person
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific, like adding 'the' in English

steps.m: those who (describing كَفَرُوا۟ - they who disbelieved)
steps.n: Added final vowels and changed spelling slightly to match Quranic writing style. This word introduces a description of the disbelievers mentioned in كَفَرُوا۟.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people did this action
This means to get ahead of others or do something before others
Added fathas (َ) after each letter to make it past tense. This shows one person did this action in the past.
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did this action instead of just one person (he)
Added optional marks (ٓ and ۟) that help with proper recitation but don't change the meaning
This word makes what comes after it stronger or more certain. It's used at the start of a sentence to emphasize what follows.

steps.m: indeed they (referring to الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ - those who disbelieved)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which refers back to 'those who disbelieved' mentioned earlier in the ayah. This connects the two parts of the sentence together.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يُعْجِزُونَ (they escape/frustrate) that comes after it in this ayah
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The u-vowel at the end shows this is a normal present tense statement

steps.m: they make (someone) weak/unable (referring to those who disbelieve)
steps.n: The word changes from 'he' to 'they' (plural) by adding ون at the end, showing multiple disbelievers trying but failing to escape Allah's plan
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to cause/make something happen'
Changed vowels and added وا to make it a command for a group
Added و at beginning to connect this command with what came before
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added هُم which means 'them' to show who the action is for. This word connects to أَعِدُّوا۟ (prepare) in the ayah, showing what to prepare for them.
This is a special word used to refer to things in a general way, like saying 'what' or 'whatever' in English
The shadda (ّ) on م makes the word stronger and more emphatic. In this ayah, it's used with استطعتم (you are able to) to mean 'whatever you are able to'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added است at the beginning to show seeking ability. The و changes to ا following Arabic sound rules.
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this. The long ا is dropped when adding past tense endings.
Changed تُ to تُم to show this is addressing multiple people
Added ا (alif) to help pronounce the word since it starts with a silent letter
Basic preposition showing source or origin

Added shadda (doubling mark) on م because it comes after a vowel at the end of the previous word. This is just for pronunciation and doesn't change the meaning. This word connects to قُوَّةٍ (power/strength) to show what kind of power.
Changes from verb 'to be strong' into the noun 'strength'
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) because this word comes after the preposition مِن in the ayah, showing it's being acted upon by the preposition
Basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. Here it connects to رِّبَاطِ (tethering) to show what should be prepared.
Adds 'and' to connect this phrase to the previous items being prepared
Changes verb into noun meaning the act or place of tethering. This word is connected to الخَيْلِ (horses) to mean specifically preparing/tethering horses for battle.
Adds emphasis to show importance of military preparation
Adds kasra ending because this word is connected to الخَيْلِ (horses) in a possessive phrase
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the basic noun form

steps.m: the horses (object of مِن in مِن رِّبَاطِ)
steps.n: Added 'al' at beginning to make it specific and kasra at end to show it follows مِن (from/of). This word is connected to رِّبَاطِ (tethering/stationing) in the phrase 'from tethering of horses'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
ت at start shows it's either 'she' or 'you' doing the action

steps.m: you all terrify (the enemy of Allah and your enemy)
steps.n: Changed from 'she' to 'you all' by adding -ūna ending. Also changed vowels to match. This word scares/terrifies the enemies mentioned right after it in the ayah.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'with it', referring back to the word قُوَّةٍ (strength) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Changes verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Adding fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of the verb تُرْهِبُونَ (you terrify/strike fear into)
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

Added kasra at the end to show possession of كِتٰبِ (book). Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes in middle of speech.
Adding shadda makes this word emphasize what comes after it. Here it emphasizes that Allah (ٱللَّهَ) is All-Knowing.
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped and the first lam gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being referred to)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being referred to in this sentence. The alif at the start becomes a wasl alif (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word.
The doubled ل (shadda) makes the meaning stronger, showing it means absolutely everything with no exceptions. The damma shows this is a noun.
The kasra at the end shows this word is being acted upon by something else. Here it's connected to عَلِيمٌۢ (All-Knowing).
The بِ at the beginning means 'with' and connects this word to عَلِيمٌۢ (All-Knowing), showing Allah is All-Knowing with everything.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to make it into a noun. This word is possessed by كُلِّ (every/all) in this ayah.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this word is indefinite (any thing) and is owned by كُلِّ (every/all). The ي is written as ى but pronounced the same.

steps.m: one who knows completely (attribute of اللَّهَ)
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a permanent and complete quality. This word describes اللَّهَ (Allah) in this ayah.
The tanween (double damma) shows this is a complete description on its own. When used for Allah, it means His knowledge is perfect and complete.
